coredata.dc:title: Competitive approach to the electrochemical detection of phosphopeptides on a porous ZrO2 thin film electrode
coredata.prism:issueName: Special issue in honor of Chinese Academician Prof. Hong-Yuan Chen for his 80th birthday
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Fei-Fei
Wang, Yue-Bo
Wang, Kang
Xia, Xing-Hua
coredata.dc:description:
A competitive adsorption strategy has been designed to electrochemically detect phosphopeptides (P-peptides) based on the specific binding between ZrO2 and P-peptides. In this method, a porous ZrO2 thin film electrodeposited on a glassy carbon electrode was used to competitively capture P-peptides from their mixture with non-phosphopeptides labelled with electrochemical probe of ferrocene (Fc-peptides). The Fc-peptides previously adsorbed on the ZrO2 film modified electrode to saturation can be replaced by the non-electroactive P-peptides by competing the binding sites on ZrO2, resulting in a decrease of peak current of Fc-peptide/ZrO2 modified electrode. The change of peak current is directly related to the concentration of the P-peptides in solution, and thus, P-peptides can be quantitatively determined. The results reveal that the decreased peak current is linear proportional to the logarithm of the concentration of P-peptides from 0.1μM to 500μM with a detection limit of 0.0934μM (S/N=3), showing the high sensitivity of the competitive biosensor. Results show that the adsorption of Fc-peptides on the modified electrode follows the first-order kinetics. The binding rate constant k
b and the dissociation rate constant k d are calculated as 49.6M−1 s−1 and 1.97×10−3 s−1, respectively. The present approach offers a useful platform for the detection of phosphopeptides and will be a promising strategy for the electrochemical study of protein post-translational modification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Competitive adsorption
Adsorption kinetics
Non-phosphopeptides
Phosphopeptides
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of porous ZrO2 thin film modified glassy carbon (ZrO2/GC) electrode
Preparation of Fc-peptide modified ZrO2/GC electrode (Fc-ZrO2/GCE)
Adsorption kinetics of peptides on the modified electrode
Competitive adsorption of P-peptides on Fc-ZrO2/GCE

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National 973 Basic Research Program ( 2012CB933800 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21205070 , 21275070 , 21275071 ), and the Qutstanding Youth Scientist Research Foundation of Shandong Province ( 2007BS02003 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of a bare glassy carbon electrode surface (A) and a porous ZrO2 thin film on a GCE (B) prepared at a constant cathodic current of 0.4mA for 800s in an aqueous electrolyte of 10.0mM ZrOCl2, 1mM CTAB and 50mM KCl.
(A) CVs of the ZrO2/GCE in a solution of 1mMK3[Fe(CN)6] and 50mM NaClO4 with different pH values of 3.0, 5.0, 6.0, 8.0 and 10.0. (B) Plot of the anodic peak current as a function of solution pH.

(A) SWVs of a ZrO2/GCE in a solution of 50mM NaClO4 (pH3) containing varied concentrations of Fc-peptides. The concentration from bottom to top: 0, 10, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 80, 100, 150, 200μM. (B) Peak current of Fc from 0.00 to 77.33μA versus the concentration of Fc-peptides. The red curve shows a fit to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm with K D =39.74μM.

(A) Evolution of SWVs as a function of adsorption time in a solution of 200μM Fc-peptide, 50mM NaClO4 (pH3). (B) Peak current as a function of adsorption time. (C) Fractional coverage of surface adsorptive Fc-peptide as a function of adsorption time. (D) Plot of ln(1 - θ) versus time.
Effect of adsorption time for P-peptides on Fc-ZrO2/GC electrode. The concentration of P-peptides was 500μM in 50mM NaClO4 (pH3).

(A) SWVs of the biosensor incubated with different concentrations of P-peptides (a-k: 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 500, 1000μM). (B) Calibration curve between the Fc peak current and the logarithm of P-peptides concentration. The parameters of SWV: f=250s−1, a=25mV, ΔE=4mV, scan rate=1V/s.
Illustration of the P-peptides electrochemical biosensor based on the specific affinity of ZrO2 to P-peptides and the competitive adsorption between Fc-peptides and P-peptides.

head.ce:title: Competitive approach to the electrochemical detection of phosphopeptides on a porous ZrO2 thin film electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255049, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A competitive adsorption strategy has been designed to electrochemically detect phosphopeptides (P-peptides) based on the specific binding between ZrO2 and P-peptides. In this method, a porous ZrO2 thin film electrodeposited on a glassy carbon electrode was used to competitively capture P-peptides from their mixture with non-phosphopeptides labelled with electrochemical probe of ferrocene (Fc-peptides). The Fc-peptides previously adsorbed on the ZrO2 film modified electrode to saturation can be replaced by the non-electroactive P-peptides by competing the binding sites on ZrO2, resulting in a decrease of peak current of Fc-peptide/ZrO2 modified electrode. The change of peak current is directly related to the concentration of the P-peptides in solution, and thus, P-peptides can be quantitatively determined. The results reveal that the decreased peak current is linear proportional to the logarithm of the concentration of P-peptides from 0.1μM to 500μM with a detection limit of 0.0934μM (S/N=3), showing the high sensitivity of the competitive biosensor. Results show that the adsorption of Fc-peptides on the modified electrode follows the first-order kinetics. The binding rate constant k
b and the dissociation rate constant k d are calculated as 49.6M−1 s−1 and 1.97×10−3 s−1, respectively. The present approach offers a useful platform for the detection of phosphopeptides and will be a promising strategy for the electrochemical study of protein post-translational modification.

A competitive adsorption strategy has been designed to electrochemically detect phosphopeptides (P-peptides) based on the specific binding between ZrO2 and P-peptides. P-peptides could replace the electroactive Fc-peptides previously adsorbed on the modified electrode. The results reveal that the decreased peak current is linear proportional to the logarithm of the concentration of P-peptides.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphorylation is a common post-translational modification that regulates various cellular activities including cellular growth, division and signal transduction [1–3]. The aberrant phosphorylation may lead to various diseases, such as cancer, leprosy and Alzheimer [4,5]. Therefore, the identification and quantification of phosphopeptides (P-peptides) as well as the related enzyme activities are vital for the study of basic biological processes and the development of diagnostic and therapeutic for diseases.

The analysis of kinase activity and the quantitative detection of P-peptides are the main methods to assess the degree of phosphorylation [6–9]. A number of techniques have been proposed for the detection of P-peptides, including mass spectrometry, fluorescence, marked radioisotopes, immunoassay approaches and electrochemistry [10–14]. Among these methods, phosphoproteins or phosphopeptides should be separated and enriched before mass spectrum detection [10]. Radiometric method generally depends on the transfer of radioactive phosphate of γ-32P from ATP to the substrate peptides [11]. Similarly, fluorescence assays need to label the substrate peptides with fluorescein such as fluorescein isothiocyanate [12]. Immunoassay approaches rely on the use of specialized biological reagents such as phosphopeptides antibodies or a protein binding domain to recognize the phosphopeptides produced by kinase reaction [13]. However, electrochemical technique can overcome many these restrictions, such as radioactive or/and fluorescence labeling, specialized antibodies, complicated operation and high cost. By using the electroactive ferrocene-conjugated ATP as the co-substrate for protein kinase C, electrochemical approach enables us to carry out activity profiling of kinases [14]. Via the electrostatic interaction, electrochemical species such as [Ru(NH3)6]3+ could sensitively assay the protein kinase activity due to the generation of phosphate groups after phosphorylation by the enzyme [15]. By making use of the affinity of ferric ions to the phosphate groups and its catalytic activity toward the electrochemical reduction of hydrogen peroxide, sensitive and selective assay of kinase activity has been reported [16]. With the progresses made in nanoscience and nanotechnologies and surface modifications, electrochemical methods can simultaneously achieve enrichment and detection of P-peptides [17]. Many enrichment techniques for P-peptides generally rely on the interaction of phosphoric moieties with metal ions or metal oxide like Fe3+, Ga3+, La3+, Ce3+, TiO2, ZrO2 and Al2O3 [18–24]. Among them, ZrO2 has been widely used for detection of P-peptides [25] and organophosphate pesticides [26]. The amphoteric property of ZrO2 benefits the binding and release of the species containing phosphate groups under different pH, which holds great promise for potential application in P-peptides isolation and enrichment [27]. It has been reported that ZrO2 films can be easily prepared by electrodeposition of ZrOCl2 at electrode surfaces directly [28].

Herein, we report a new approach for electrochemical detection of phosphopeptides on a porous ZrO2 thin film electrode. This is the first application of competitive adsorption in electrochemical detection of P-peptides utilizing nonspecific adsorption of electrochemical probe labelled nonphosphopeptides. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , a porous ZrO2 thin film is electrochemically deposited on glassy carbon electrode (GCE) at cathodic potentials. Ferrocence labelled non-phosphopeptides (Fc-peptides) electrochemical probe can nonspecifically adsorb on the obtained ZrO2 film modified electrode, exhibiting an obvious current peak of the Fc moieties. Quantification of P-peptides can be realized by a competitive process between Fc-peptides and P-peptides to the active sites of ZrO2. Due to the specific affinity of ZrO2 to P-peptides, previously adsorbed Fc-peptides can be competitively replaced by the non-electroactive P-peptides, which results in a decrease of the peak current. Therefore, P-peptides can be sensitively detected by monitoring the change of Fc peak current.

The Ferrocene marked nonphosphopeptides (Fc-LRRASLGGGGC, peptide purity>95%) were synthesized by Apeptide Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and the phosphopeptides (LQQH(p-Y)G, peptide purity>95%) were obtained from China Peptides Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Zirconium oxychloride octahydrate (ZrOCl2·8H2O, reagent grade>98%) and Hexadecyltrimethyl-ammonium bromide (CTAB, reagent grade>96%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The pH of 50mM NaClO4 electrolyte was adjusted by dilute NaOH or HClO4 solution. The modified electrode was thoroughly rinsed with the same solution before each test. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18.3 MΩ·cm) from the Millipore Milli-Q system. All the experiments were conducted at room temperature.

Chronopotentiometry, cyclic voltammetry and square-wave voltammetry measurements were performed on a CHI 660 electrochemical analyzer (CH Instruments, Austin, TX). The three-electrode electrochemical system consisted of a glassy carbon electrode (3-mm diameter) as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode and a platinum wire as the reference and working electrodes, respectively. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained from a Hitachi S4800 scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of porous ZrO2 thin film modified glassy carbon (ZrO2/GC) electrode
ce:section.ce:para: As we reported previously, hydrogen bubble generated at the cathodic potentials due to the reduction of water can be used as dynamic template to prepare porous structure at electrode surface [29,30]. Recently, it was reported that ZrO2 films could be prepared by electrochemical reduction of ZrOCl2 at a bare gold surface [31]. Inspired by these methods, we prepared porous ZrO2 thin films on GCE by cathodic electrodeposition method. Before electrodeposition, the GCE (3mm in diameter) was polished with 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05μm alumina slurries and sequentially sonicated for 3min in ethanol and ultrapure water, respectively. Then, ZrO2 was deposited onto the bare GCE at a constant cathodic current of 0.4mA (current density of 5.7mA·cm−2) for 800s in an aqueous solution of 10.0mM ZrOCl2, 1mM CTAB and 50mM KCl. Finally, the modified electrode was rinsed with water and dried with N2 for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Fc-peptide modified ZrO2/GC electrode (Fc-ZrO2/GCE)
ce:section.ce:para: The ZrO2/GCE was coated with 8μL of 0.2mM Fc-peptides solution (50mmol/L NaClO4, pH3) and kept for 5min. After the electrode was rinsed carefully with blank perchlorate buffer solution (pH3), the Fc-peptides modified ZrO2/GC electrode (Fc-ZrO2/GCE) was obtained.

Cyclic voltammogram was employed to study the amphoteric properties of ZrO2/GCE. CVs were carried out from −0.1 to 0.6V at a scan rate of 50mV/s in a solution of 1mMK3[Fe(CN)6] and 50mM NaClO4. The solution pH value was adjusted by the dilute NaOH or HClO4 solution.

Fc-ZrO2/GCE was immersed into a P-peptides solution with a desired concentration for 60min. Then, the electrode was rinsed thoroughly and transferred into an electrolyte to detect P-peptide. CVs were performed from 0.3 to 0.8V with a scan rate of 1V/s in the electrolyte of 50mM NaClO4 solution (pH3).

The mechanism of electrodeposited porous ZrO2 thin film can be described as follows [31]:
(1)
2
H 2
O + 2
e −
→
H 2
↑ + 2
OH −
(2)
ZrO
2 +
+
H 2
O
→
O
H −
Zr
OH
4
(3)
Zr
OH
4
→ Z
rO 2
+ 2
H 2
O

When a constant cathodic current of 0.4mA (current density of 5.7mA·cm−2, 800s) is applied to the glassy carbon electrode, water molecules will be electrochemically reduced to hydrogen bubbles and hydroxyl ions (as shown in Fig. S-1). The hydrogen bubbles produced at the electrode surface function as the dynamic template for porous structure formation, and the hydroxyl ions acting as the catalyst to accelerate the hydrolysis of ZrO2+ to Zr(OH)4 which is transferred to ZrO2 via dehydration process. Fig. 1 shows the typical SEM image of a porous ZrO2 thin film on a GCE prepared at a constant cathodic current of 0.4mA. The formed ZrO2 film is relatively smooth containing irregular porous structure which could offer a large electrode surface area and enhance the sensitivity of electrochemical detection by increasing the interacting opportunities of P-peptides and ZrO2.

ZrO2 is known to have amphoteric properties of Lewis acid and base according to the different pH of solution [27]. It can react as a Lewis acid which may contribute to the binding affinity toward P-peptides. Thus, the prepared surface property of the ZrO2/GCE was studied by electrochemical titration method using K3[Fe(CN)6] redox probe. Fig. 2 shows the typical cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of the redox probe on a ZrO2/GCE and the anodic peak current changes with solution pH. Consistent with the reported results [32], at lower pH (<4), ZrO2 behaves as a Lewis acid carrying positive charges, displaying the electrostatic attraction to the probes. With the increase of pH (4–10), ZrO2 behaves as a Lewis base and the ZrO2/GCE carries negative charges which repel the access of the negatively charged electrochemical probe via the electrostatic interactions, resulting in the decrease in peak current. From the plot of peak current as a function of solution pH, an isoelectric point of the ZrO2/GCE can be estimated as 5.0±0.1. Given the stability of ferrocene, we finally choose the 50mM NaClO4 (pH3) as the subsequent detection solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption kinetics of peptides on the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
The adsorption kinetics of Fc-peptides on the ZrO2/GCE was monitored electrochemically. As shown in Fig. 3A, the peak current of Fc in the square-wave voltammetry (SWV) increases with the concentration of Fc-peptides in solution. The relationship between the interfacial adsorption and Fc-peptides concentration is plotted in Fig. 3B. From 0 to 50μM, the peak current increases sharply and then gradually reaches equilibrium state at high concentration assuming a full monolayer of Fc-peptides is formed on the ZrO2 surface at 200μM. The results indicate that the adsorption of Fc-peptides on the ZrO2 film may follow the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, which has the form of Eq. (4) [33]:
(4)
A =
A max
C
Fc − peptides
K D
+
C
Fc − peptides
where, K D is the dissociation constant of Fc-peptides on ZrO2 film; A and A
max refer to the interfacial absorption coverage for a given concentration (CFc-peptides) and at the saturation level, respectively. Fitting the data (the red line in Fig. 3B) gives A
max of 85.97 and K D of 39.74μM. These values indicate a high affinity between Fc-peptides and ZrO2, which provides the basis for competitive adsorption and quantitative determination of P-peptides.

As shown in Fig. 4A, the adsorption kinetics of 200μM Fc-peptides on ZrO2/GC electrode was monitored by the CVs in real time. It shows that the dynamical response current increases with the increase of adsorption time in Fc-peptides solution and reaches an equilibrium state at ~300s (Fig. 4B). The observed rapid adsorption of Fc-peptides may be ascribed to the large surface area and porous structure of ZrO2 thin film.

To evaluate the adsorption kinetics at a surface, we assume that the intramolecular interaction among the Fc-peptides bound on GCE surface can be neglected. According to the traditional two-compartment model, the adsorption rate can be expressed as Eq. (5):
(5)
dθ dt
=
k b
1 − θ
C 0
−
k d
θ
where, θ denotes the fractional coverage of surface immobilized Fc-peptides, C 0 is the concentration of Fc-peptides in solution, k
b and k d denote the binding and dissociation rate constants, respectively. The term k d is negligible, because the peak current of Fc remains almost constant for several hours after rinsing with blank perchlorate buffer solution (Fig. S-2). Eq. (5) can then be simplified and its analytical solution is expressed as Eq. (6):
(6)
ln
1 − θ
= −
k b
C 0
t + C
where, C is a constant. Plots of θ and ln(1-θ) as function of time are shown in Fig. 4C and D, respectively. Clearly, the ln(1-θ) value as a function of reaction time decreases linearly as revealed by a straight line, which demonstrates that the surface adsorption follows the first-order kinetics. The binding rate constant k
b is calculated as 49.6M−1 s−1 by the fitting line in Fig. 4D. The dissociation rate constant k d is calculated to be 1.97×10−3 s−1 according to the equation of K D = k d/k
b .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Competitive adsorption of P-peptides on Fc-ZrO2/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The strong affinity between ZrO2 and phosphoric moieties indeed provides a label-free biosensor platform for quantification of P-peptides. We used this specific binding of P-peptides on the active sites ZrO2 to replace the Fc-peptides previously adsorbed on ZrO2/GCE. First, we measured the decrease of peak current for Fc-peptides due to the competitive adsorption of P-peptides from solution as a function of competitive adsorption time. As shown in Fig. 5 , the peak current decreases rapidly within the first 50min for competitive adsorption, then it reaches a steady-state at 60min. With the further increase of competitive adsorption time, the peak current does not change, which demonstrates that the competitive adsorption reaches equilibrium at adsorption time longer than 60min. Fig. 6A shows the SWVs of the Fc-ZrO2/GCE as a function of P-peptides concentration after 60min competitive adsorption. The peak current decreases gradually with increasing the concentration of P-peptides in a range of 0.1–500μM. Fig. 6B depicts the linear correlation between the peak current and the logarithm of P-peptides concentration with a correlation coefficient of 0.993. The regression equation is I =59.546–13.136 × (x is the logarithm of P-peptides concentration). The detection limit is estimated to be 0.0934μM (S/N=3), which is lower than that of most other electrochemical methods [34].

In addition, the reuse of modified electrode was examined. It has been reported that the P-peptides adsorbed on mesoporous zirconium oxide nanomaterials could be eluted at a high pH [22]. While, acetonitrile (ACN) can eliminate the hydrophobic interaction between metal oxides and peptides [35]. In our experiments, the modified electrode previously adsorbed with peptide mixtures was eluted with 0.5% NH3·H2O in 60% ACN (pH11.5). The results demonstrate that the Fc current remains only 4.25% when the modified electrode was eluted for 3 times (Fig. S-3). After that, the modified electrode was rinsed carefully and dried with N2 for Fc-peptides adsorption again. The peak current for Fc reaches to 86.33% of the original value, which indicates that the modified electrode can be reused with a good reproducibility.

In summary, we demonstrated a competitive strategy of electrochemical sensing protocol for detection of phosphopeptides utilizing the strong affinity between ZrO2 and phosphoric group. The porous ZrO2 thin film modified glassy carbon electrode was prepared successfully by a hydrogen bubble template method. The porous structure of ZrO2 has large surface area for peptides adsorption and improves the mass transport which enhances the detection sensitivity. Compared with the traditional methods, this method proposes a competitive electrochemical idea of Fc-peptides and P-peptides fighting for the binding sites on ZrO2 and makes a good use of the nonspecific adsorption of nonphosphopeptides. Furthermore, the concept can be used as a general platform to quantify phosphopeptides that are phosphorylated on either threonine or serine.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National 973 Basic Research Program (2012CB933800), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21205070, 21275070, 21275071), and the Qutstanding Youth Scientist Research Foundation of Shandong Province (2007BS02003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2016.11.001.
